message.content:
You are an adversarial security reviewer. Your job is to REFUTE the following candidate vulnerability. Default to refuted=true unless the evidence is solid. Read the actual code at the cited path before deciding.

TARGET: chia-blockchain daemon keychain RPC  (repo root: C:/bugBounty/targets/chia-blockchain daemon keychain RPC)
THREAT MODEL: The daemon exposes a local WebSocket control server (server.py) plus keychain_server.py/keychain_proxy.py. Threat: a process or web page able to reach the daemon websocket, or weak auth, leading to command/argument injection when launching plotters/services (subprocess), arbitrary command exec, path traversal in file ops, or keychain access without authorization. Check websocket origin/auth validation and how command names/args from messages reach subprocess.

CANDIDATE FINDING:
  title: Client-controlled kc_user/kc_service select arbitrary keychain partition (no per-client isolation)
  class: missing authorization / keychain access (CWE-862)
  severity claimed: Info
  file: chia/daemon/keychain_server.py:154
  source: WebSocket message data fields request["kc_user"] and request["kc_service"], fully attacker-controllable in a raw message
  sink: Keychain(user=user, service=service) construction in get_keychain_for_request, used by all key read/write/delete operations
  data_flow: handle_message -> KeychainServer.handle_command -> e.g. get_all_private_keys/delete_all_keys -> get_keychain_for_request(request): user=request.get('kc_user'), service=request.get('kc_service') -> new Keychain(user,service). Because the partition is taken verbatim from the request, any authenticated client can target the default keychain or any other partition; partitioning provides no security boundary between clients sharing the node cert. delete_all_keys with an attacker-chosen partition is a destructive primitive.
  excerpt: user = request.get('kc_user', self._default_keychain.user)
service = request.get('kc_service', self._default_keychain.service)
...
keychain = Keychain(user=user, service=service)

LENS = CONCRETE EXPLOIT. Construct the precise input/request/payload that triggers the issue and state the resulting impact. If you cannot produce a plausible working exploit from the actual code (or it requires unrealistic preconditions), set refuted=true.

Set is_real=true ONLY if, after reading the code, the finding survives your lens. Provide reason and, if real, an exploit_sketch and adjusted_severity. Return ONLY the structured object.

- deep-research: Deep research harness — fan-out web searches, fetch sources, adversarially verify claims, synthesize a cited report. - When the user wants a deep, multi-source, fact-checked research report on any topic. BEFORE invoking, check if the question is specific enough to research directly — if underspecified (e.g., "what car to buy" without budget/use-case/region), ask 2-3 clarifying questions to narrow scope. Then pass the refined question as args, weaving the answers in.
- contribute:contribute: This skill should be used when the user asks to "contribute to open source", "find an issue to work on", "analyze a repo", "submit a pull request", "review PR feedback", "triage an issue", "sync my fork", "create a release", "clean up contribution branches", "debug CI failures", or mentions open-source contribution workflows. Provides a full-lifecycle open-source contribution system with 12 phases.
- update-config: Use this skill to configure the Claude Code harness via settings.json. Automated behaviors ("from now on when X", "each time X", "whenever X", "before/after X") require hooks configured in settings.json - the harness executes these, not Claude, so memory/preferences cannot fulfill them. Also use for: permissions ("allow X", "add permission", "move permission to"), env vars ("set X=Y"), hook troubleshooting, or any changes to settings.json/settings.local.json files. Examples: "allow npm commands", "add bq permission to global settings", "move permission to user settings", "set DEBUG=true", "when claude stops show X". For simple settings like theme/model, suggest the /config command.
- keybindings-help: Use when the user wants to customize keyboard shortcuts, rebind keys, add chord bindings, or modify ~/.claude/keybindings.json. Examples: "rebind ctrl+s", "add a chord shortcut", "change the submit key", "customize keybindings".
- verify: Verify that a code change actually does what it's supposed to by running the app and observing behavior. Use when asked to verify a PR, confirm a fix works, test a change manually, check that a feature works, or validate local changes before pushing.
- code-review: Review the current diff for correctness bugs and reuse/simplification/efficiency cleanups at the given effort level (low/medium: fewer, high-confidence findings; high→max: broader coverage, may include uncertain findings). Pass --comment to post findings as inline PR comments, or --fix to apply the findings to the working tree after the review.
- simplify: Review the changed code for reuse, simplification, efficiency, and altitude cleanups, then apply the fixes. Quality only — it does not hunt for bugs; use /code-review for that.
- fewer-permission-prompts: Scan your transcripts for common read-only Bash and MCP tool calls, then add a prioritized allowlist to project .claude/settings.json to reduce permission prompts.
- loop: Run a prompt or slash command on a recurring interval (e.g. /loop 5m /foo). Omit the interval to let the model self-pace. - When the user wants to set up a recurring task, poll for status, or run something repeatedly on an interval (e.g. "check the deploy every 5 minutes", "keep running /babysit-prs"). Do NOT invoke for one-off tasks.
- schedule: Create, update, list, or run scheduled cloud agents (routines) that execute on a cron schedule. - When the user wants to schedule a recurring cloud agent, set up automated tasks, create a cron job for Claude Code, or manage their scheduled agents/routines. Also use when the user wants a one-time scheduled run ("run this once at 3pm", "remind me to check X tomorrow").
- claude-api: Reference for the Claude API / Anthropic SDK — model ids, pricing, params, streaming, tool use, MCP, agents, caching, token counting, model migration.
TRIGGER — read BEFORE opening the target file; don't skip because it "looks like a one-liner" — whenever: the prompt names Claude/Anthropic in any form (Claude, Anthropic, Fable, Opus, Sonnet, Haiku, `anthropic`, `@anthropic-ai`, `claude-*`, `us.anthropic.*`, `[1m]`); the user asks about an LLM (pricing/model choice/limits/caching) — never answer from memory; OR the task is LLM-shaped with provider unstated (agent/MCP/tool-definition/multi-agent/RAG/LLM-judge/computer-use; generate/summarize/extract/classify/rewrite/converse over NL; debugging refusals/cutoffs/streaming/tool-calls/tokens).
SKIP only when another provider is being worked on (overrides all triggers): OpenAI/GPT/Gemini/Llama/Mistral/Cohere/Ollama named in the query; OR `grep -rE 'openai|langchain_openai|google.generativeai|genai|mistralai|cohere|ollama'` over the project hits (run this grep FIRST if no provider named — don't Read the file).
- run: Launch and drive this project's app to see a change working. Use when asked to run, start, or screenshot the app, or to confirm a change works in the real app (not just tests). First looks for a project skill that already covers launching the app; otherwise falls back to built-in patterns per project type (CLI, server, TUI, Electron, browser-driven, library).
- init: Initialize a new CLAUDE.md file with codebase documentation
- review: Review a pull request
- security-review: Complete a security review of the pending changes on the current branch

content.content:
1	from __future__ import annotations
2	
3	import logging
4	from dataclasses import dataclass, field
5	from pathlib import Path
6	from typing import Any
7	
8	from chia_rs import PrivateKey
9	from chia_rs.sized_ints import uint32
10	
11	from chia.cmds.init_funcs import check_keys
12	from chia.util.errors import KeychainException, KeychainFingerprintNotFound
13	from chia.util.keychain import Keychain, KeyData
14	from chia.util.streamable import Streamable, streamable
15	
16	# Commands that are handled by the KeychainServer
17	keychain_commands = [
18	    "add_private_key",
19	    "add_key",
20	    "check_keys",
21	    "delete_all_keys",
22	    "delete_key_by_fingerprint",
23	    "get_all_private_keys",
24	    "get_first_private_key",
25	    "get_key_for_fingerprint",
26	    "get_key",
27	    "get_keys",
28	    "get_public_key",
29	    "get_public_keys",
30	    "set_label",
31	    "delete_label",
32	]
33	
34	log = logging.getLogger(__name__)
35	
36	KEYCHAIN_ERR_KEYERROR = "key error"
37	KEYCHAIN_ERR_LOCKED = "keyring is locked"
38	KEYCHAIN_ERR_NO_KEYS = "no keys present"
39	KEYCHAIN_ERR_KEY_NOT_FOUND = "key not found"
40	KEYCHAIN_ERR_MALFORMED_REQUEST = "malformed request"
41	
42	
43	@streamable
44	@dataclass(frozen=True)
45	class EmptyResponse(Streamable):
46	    pass
47	
48	
49	@streamable
50	@dataclass(frozen=True)
51	class GetKeyResponse(Streamable):
52	    key: KeyData
53	
54	
55	@streamable
56	@dataclass(frozen=True)
57	class GetKeyRequest(Streamable):
58	    fingerprint: uint32
59	    include_secrets: bool = False
60	
61	    def run(self, keychain: Keychain) -> GetKeyResponse:
62	        return GetKeyResponse(key=keychain.get_key(self.fingerprint, self.include_secrets))
63	
64	
65	@streamable
66	@dataclass(frozen=True)
67	class GetKeysResponse(Streamable):
68	    keys: list[KeyData]
69	
70	
71	@streamable
72	@dataclass(frozen=True)
73	class GetKeysRequest(Streamable):
74	    include_secrets: bool = False
75	
76	    def run(self, keychain: Keychain) -> GetKeysResponse:
77	        return GetKeysResponse(keys=keychain.get_keys(self.include_secrets))
78	
79	
80	@streamable
81	@dataclass(frozen=True)
82	class GetPublicKeyRequest(Streamable):
83	    fingerprint: uint32
84	
85	    def run(self, keychain: Keychain) -> GetPublicKeyResponse:
86	        return GetPublicKeyResponse(key=keychain.get_key(self.fingerprint, include_secrets=False))
87	
88	
89	@streamable
90	@dataclass(frozen=True)
91	class GetPublicKeyResponse(Streamable):
92	    key: KeyData
93	
94	    def to_json_dict(self) -> dict[str, Any]:
95	        # Ensure that only approved keys are returned
96	        approved_keys = ["fingerprint", "public_key", "label"]
97	        key_dict = self.key.to_json_dict()
98	        return {"key": {key: key_dict[key] for key in approved_keys if key in key_dict}}
99	
100	
101	@streamable
102	@dataclass(frozen=True)
103	class GetPublicKeysRequest(Streamable):
104	    def run(self, keychain: Keychain) -> GetPublicKeysResponse:
105	        return GetPublicKeysResponse(keys=keychain.get_keys(include_secrets=False))
106	
107	
108	@streamable
109	@dataclass(frozen=True)
110	class GetPublicKeysResponse(Streamable):
111	    keys: list[KeyData]
112	
113	    def to_json_dict(self) -> dict[str, Any]:
114	        # Ensure that only approved keys are returned
115	        approved_keys = ["fingerprint", "public_key", "label"]
116	        return {
117	            "keys": [
118	                {key: key_data_dict[key] for key in approved_keys if key in key_data_dict}
119	                for key_data_dict in (key_data.to_json_dict() for key_data in self.keys)
120	            ]
121	        }
122	
123	
124	@streamable
125	@dataclass(frozen=True)
126	class SetLabelRequest(Streamable):
127	    fingerprint: uint32
128	    label: str
129	
130	    def run(self, keychain: Keychain) -> EmptyResponse:
131	        keychain.set_label(int(self.fingerprint), self.label)
132	        return EmptyResponse()
133	
134	
135	@streamable
136	@dataclass(frozen=True)
137	class DeleteLabelRequest(Streamable):
138	    fingerprint: uint32
139	
140	    def run(self, keychain: Keychain) -> EmptyResponse:
141	        keychain.delete_label(self.fingerprint)
142	        return EmptyResponse()
143	
144	
145	@dataclass
146	class KeychainServer:
147	    """
148	    Implements a remote keychain service for clients to perform key operations on
149	    """
150	
151	    _default_keychain: Keychain = field(default_factory=Keychain)
152	    _alt_keychains: dict[str, Keychain] = field(default_factory=dict)
153	
154	    def get_keychain_for_request(self, request: dict[str, Any]) -> Keychain:
155	        """
156	        Keychain instances can have user and service strings associated with them.
157	        The keychain backends ultimately point to the same data stores, but the user
158	        and service strings are used to partition those data stores. We attempt to
159	        maintain a mapping of user/service pairs to their corresponding Keychain.
160	        """
161	        user = request.get("kc_user", self._default_keychain.user)
162	        service = request.get("kc_service", self._default_keychain.service)
163	        if user == self._default_keychain.user and service == self._default_keychain.service:
164	            keychain = self._default_keychain
165	        else:
166	            key = (user or "unnamed") + (service or "")
167	            if key in self._alt_keychains:
168	                keychain = self._alt_keychains[key]
169	            else:
170	                keychain = Keychain(user=user, service=service)
171	                self._alt_keychains[key] = keychain
172	        return keychain
173	
174	    async def handle_command(self, command: str, data: dict[str, Any]) -> dict[str, Any]:
175	        try:
176	            if command == "add_private_key":
177	                data["private"] = True
178	                data["mnemonic_or_pk"] = data.get("mnemonic_or_pk", data.get("mnemonic", None))
179	                return await self.add_key(data)
180	            elif command == "add_key":
181	                return await self.add_key(data)
182	            elif command == "check_keys":
183	                return await self.check_keys(data)
184	            elif command == "delete_all_keys":
185	                return await self.delete_all_keys(data)
186	            elif command == "delete_key_by_fingerprint":
187	                return await self.delete_key_by_fingerprint(data)
188	            elif command == "get_all_private_keys":
189	                return await self.get_all_private_keys(data)
190	            elif command == "get_first_private_key":
191	                return await self.get_first_private_key(data)
192	            elif command == "get_key_for_fingerprint":
193	                return await self.get_key_for_fingerprint(data)
194	            elif command == "get_key":
195	                return await self.run_request(data, GetKeyRequest)
196	            elif command == "get_keys":
197	                return await self.run_request(data, GetKeysRequest)
198	            elif command == "get_public_key":
199	                return await self.run_request(data, GetPublicKeyRequest)
200	            elif command == "get_public_keys":
201	                return await self.run_request(data, GetPublicKeysRequest)
202	            elif command == "set_label":
203	                return await self.run_request(data, SetLabelRequest)
204	            elif command == "delete_label":
205	                return await self.run_request(data, DeleteLabelRequest)
206	            return {}
207	        except Exception as e:
208	            log.exception(e)
209	            return {"success": False, "error": str(e), "command": command}
210	
211	    async def add_key(self, request: dict[str, Any]) -> dict[str, Any]:
212	        if self.get_keychain_for_request(request).is_keyring_locked():
213	            return {"success": False, "error": KEYCHAIN_ERR_LOCKED}
214	
215	        mnemonic_or_pk = request.get("mnemonic_or_pk", None)
216	        label = request.get("label", None)
217	        private = request.get("private", True)
218	
219	        if mnemonic_or_pk is None:
220	            return {
221	                "success": False,
222	                "error": KEYCHAIN_ERR_MALFORMED_REQUEST,
223	                "error_details": {"message": "missing key information"},
224	            }
225	
226	        try:
227	            key = self.get_keychain_for_request(request).add_key(mnemonic_or_pk, label, private)
228	        except KeyError as e:
229	            return {
230	                "success": False,
231	                "error": KEYCHAIN_ERR_KEYERROR,
232	                "error_details": {"message": f"The word '{e.args[0]}' is incorrect.'", "word": e.args[0]},
233	            }
234	        except ValueError as e:
235	            log.exception(e)
236	            return {
237	                "success": False,
238	                "error": str(e),
239	            }
240	
241	        if isinstance(key, PrivateKey):
242	            fingerprint = key.get_g1().get_fingerprint()
243	        else:
244	            fingerprint = key.get_fingerprint()
245	
246	        return {"success": True, "fingerprint": fingerprint}
247	
248	    async def check_keys(self, request: dict[str, Any]) -> dict[str, Any]:
249	        if self.get_keychain_for_request(request).is_keyring_locked():
250	            return {"success": False, "error": KEYCHAIN_ERR_LOCKED}
251	
252	        root_path = request.get("root_path", None)
253	        if root_path is None:
254	            return {
255	                "success": False,
256	                "error": KEYCHAIN_ERR_MALFORMED_REQUEST,
257	                "error_details": {"message": "missing root_path"},
258	            }
259	
260	        check_keys(Path(root_path))
261	
262	        return {"success": True}
263	
264	    async def delete_all_keys(self, request: dict[str, Any]) -> dict[str, Any]:
265	        if self.get_keychain_for_request(request).is_keyring_locked():
266	            return {"success": False, "error": KEYCHAIN_ERR_LOCKED}
267	
268	        self.get_keychain_for_request(request).delete_all_keys()
269	
270	        return {"success": True}
271	
272	    async def delete_key_by_fingerprint(self, request: dict[str, Any]) -> dict[str, Any]:
273	        if self.get_keychain_for_request(request).is_keyring_locked():
274	            return {"success": False, "error": KEYCHAIN_ERR_LOCKED}
275	
276	        fingerprint = request.get("fingerprint", None)
277	        if fingerprint is None:
278	            return {
279	                "success": False,
280	                "error": KEYCHAIN_ERR_MALFORMED_REQUEST,
281	                "error_details": {"message": "missing fingerprint"},
282	            }
283	
284	        self.get_keychain_for_request(request).delete_key_by_fingerprint(fingerprint)
285	
286	        return {"success": True}
287	
288	    async def run_request(self, request_dict: dict[str, Any], request_type: type[Any]) -> dict[str, Any]:
289	        keychain = self.get_keychain_for_request(request_dict)
290	        if keychain.is_keyring_locked():
291	            return {"success": False, "error": KEYCHAIN_ERR_LOCKED}
292	
293	        try:
294	            request = request_type.from_json_dict(request_dict)
295	        except Exception as e:
296	            return {
297	                "success": False,
298	                "error": KEYCHAIN_ERR_MALFORMED_REQUEST,
299	                "error_details": {"message": str(e)},
300	            }
301	
302	        try:
303	            return {"success": True, **request.run(keychain).to_json_dict()}
304	        except KeychainFingerprintNotFound as e:
305	            return {
306	                "success": False,
307	                "error": KEYCHAIN_ERR_KEY_NOT_FOUND,
308	                "error_details": {"fingerprint": e.fingerprint},
309	            }
310	        except KeychainException as e:
311	            return {
312	                "success": False,
313	                "error": KEYCHAIN_ERR_MALFORMED_REQUEST,
314	                "error_details": {"message": str(e)},
315	            }
316	
317	    async def get_all_private_keys(self, request: dict[str, Any]) -> dict[str, Any]:
318	        all_keys: list[dict[str, Any]] = []
319	        if self.get_keychain_for_request(request).is_keyring_locked():
320	            return {"success": False, "error": KEYCHAIN_ERR_LOCKED}
321	
322	        private_keys = self.get_keychain_for_request(request).get_all_private_keys()
323	        for sk, entropy in private_keys:
324	            all_keys.append({"pk": bytes(sk.get_g1()).hex(), "entropy": entropy.hex()})
325	
326	        return {"success": True, "private_keys": all_keys}
327	
328	    async def get_first_private_key(self, request: dict[str, Any]) -> dict[str, Any]:
329	        key: dict[str, Any] = {}
330	        if self.get_keychain_for_request(request).is_keyring_locked():
331	            return {"success": False, "error": KEYCHAIN_ERR_LOCKED}
332	
333	        sk_ent = self.get_keychain_for_request(request).get_first_private_key()
334	        if sk_ent is None:
335	            return {"success": False, "error": KEYCHAIN_ERR_NO_KEYS}
336	
337	        pk_str = bytes(sk_ent[0].get_g1()).hex()
338	        ent_str = sk_ent[1].hex()
339	        key = {"pk": pk_str, "entropy": ent_str}
340	
341	        return {"success": True, "private_key": key}
342	
343	    async def get_key_for_fingerprint(self, request: dict[str, Any]) -> dict[str, Any]:
344	        keychain = self.get_keychain_for_request(request)
345	        if keychain.is_keyring_locked():
346	            return {"success": False, "error": KEYCHAIN_ERR_LOCKED}
347	
348	        private = request.get("private", True)
349	        keys = keychain.get_keys(include_secrets=private)
350	        if len(keys) == 0:
351	            return {"success": False, "error": KEYCHAIN_ERR_NO_KEYS}
352	
353	        try:
354	            if request["fingerprint"] is None:
355	                key_data = keys[0]
356	            else:
357	                key_data = keychain.get_key(request["fingerprint"], include_secrets=private)
358	        except KeychainFingerprintNotFound:
359	            return {"success": False, "error": KEYCHAIN_ERR_KEY_NOT_FOUND}
360	
361	        return {
362	            "success": True,
363	            "pk": bytes(key_data.public_key).hex(),
364	            "entropy": key_data.entropy.hex() if private else None,
365	        }
366

content.text:
The code matches the finding. Now the key question for my lens: is this a real security boundary that's being bypassed? The finding itself claims severity "Info" and notes "partitioning provides no security boundary between clients sharing the node cert." Let me check the threat model. The candidate explicitly admits partitioning is NOT a security boundary. Any client that can reach the keychain server already shares the node cert and can do everything. Choosing a partition doesn't cross a privilege boundary — there are no other clients with separate partitions to attack.

This is by design: the user/service strings partition keyring data stores, not isolate clients. There is no per-client identity in this RPC at all; auth is purely the shared cert. So "select arbitrary partition" gives no additional capability — the same client could already read/delete the default keychain.

This is not a vulnerability; it's working as designed, and the finding's own narrative concedes it's not a security boundary.

input.reason: Code at keychain_server.py:154-172 matches the excerpt: kc_user/kc_service are taken verbatim from the request and used to construct/select a Keychain. However, this is not a security vulnerability. The user/service strings are documented (and function as) a data-store PARTITIONING mechanism, not an access-control boundary. The keychain RPC has no concept of per-client identity at all: authentication is solely the shared node TLS cert, so every authenticated client is equally privileged and already has full access to the default keychain (read all private keys, delete_all_keys, etc.). Letting a client name a different partition grants no capability the client did not already possess against the default partition, and there is no second, differently-privileged client whose partition could be 'stolen' or destroyed across a boundary. The finding's own data_flow narrative concedes 'partitioning provides no security boundary between clients sharing the node cert' — i.e., it is admitting there is no boundary to cross. delete_all_keys with an attacker-chosen partition is no more a 'destructive primitive' than delete_all_keys on the default keychain, which the same caller can already invoke. No privilege escalation, no isolation bypass, no traversal (Keychain backend keys are namespaced strings, not filesystem paths). The real (separate) risk surface is whether an unauthorized party can reach the websocket at all — that is the auth/origin question, not this partition-selection behavior. As scoped, this is behavior-as-designed at Info with no concrete exploit that crosses a trust boundary.
input.exploit_sketch: No meaningful exploit. A client authenticated to the daemon websocket could send {"command":"delete_all_keys","data":{"kc_user":"x","kc_service":"y"}} to target an arbitrary partition, but that same client can already call delete_all_keys/get_all_private_keys against the default keychain. No additional privilege, no other principal's data is protected by the partition, so no security boundary is crossed.

content.text: Refuted. The cited code matches, and the data fields are attacker-controllable, but the user/service partitioning is a data-store namespacing mechanism, not an access-control boundary. The keychain RPC has no per-client identity—auth is the shared node cert—so any authenticated client is already fully privileged against the default keychain. Selecting a different partition confers no capability the client lacked. The finding's own narrative concedes "partitioning provides no security boundary," which is exactly why there is no boundary to bypass.
